Said R. Hiya: ‘It is written: “Whoso sheddeth man’s blood, by man shall his blood be shed; for in the image of God made he man” (Gen. 9, 6). He who sheds the blood of a fellow-man is thus considered as diminishing the Divine archetype of man as well. Thus the first commandment, “I am the Lord thy God”, contains the motive for the sixth, “Thou shalt not murder.”

matches.lb: Second Temple: Concerning Noah's Work as a Planter 1:1
matches.x: CONCERNING NOAH’s WORK AS A PLANTER [1] We have said in the former book all that the occasion called for regarding the husbandman’s art in general. In this book we shall give such an account as we can of the art of a vine-dresser in particular. For Moses introduces the righteous man not as a husbandman only, but specially as a vine-dresser; his words are: “Noah began to be a husbandman tilling…

And Noah, the husbandman, began and planted a vineyard (Gen. 9:20). Noah degraded himself when he began to till the soil. R. Judah the son of R. Shalum said: At first Noah was called a righteous and perfect man, but now he is described as a man of the earth. And he planted a vineyard; that is, after he planted the vineyard he was called a husbandman.

Cham the father of Canaan saw the nakedness of his father, and told it to his brothers [who were] Outside [in the marketplace].

“Ḥam, father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brothers outside” (Genesis 9:22). “Ḥam, father of Canaan, saw [and told [vayaged]]” – he said it to them and spoke persuasively to them;28Vayaged is interpreted in its Aramaic sense, of causing something to move forward, in this case, Ham’s brothers’ opinions. he said to them: Adam the first man had [only] two sons and one…

Ham, father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father. Canaan is mentioned here due to his involvement in the affair, as maintained by the Sages.
